body_txt: The protagonists are walking down a hallway or simply just relaxing and minding their own business when suddenly out of nowhere one of them is killed in a horrific, often violent manner. This form of demise can lead to The Reveal and can often lead to the confrontation with the Big Bad . Typically includes or is accompanied by Impaled with Extreme Prejudice . Sometimes is foreshadowed very briefly by the killer being shown just before the character's death. Most commonly associated with horror, where typically the responsible party is of the monster variety. It can also be used to reveal any obstacles in the protagonist's way by killing a less important character or a Red Shirt , if there are any. Not limited to villains, however, as it can also be done as part of a Mook Horror Show . When main or otherwise relevant characters are involved, this becomes a notorious subversion of the Inverse Dialogue/Death Rule and Wring Every Last Drop out of Him , as those types of characters tend to spew several lines of dialogue before kicking it. Like with nearly every trope, this trope can be done well or poorly . On one hand, killing a major character abruptly can highlight just how dangerous and fragile the setting/situation is for them; but on the other, it can be too spontaneous and anticlimactic for the narrative's benefit, and the audience might not accept it. In the latter cases, the narrative is essentially just dropping a bridge rather than reminding the audience that anyone can die . Likewise, it's best to use this trope as sparingly as possible. Killing a single character or two abruptly can advance the narrative in interesting ways, but using the same or a similar tactic over-and-over again to kill characters (especially in non-Horror settings) will eventually wear out the shock-value, and might cause the audience to lose interest since every characters will die spontaneously. Compare with Dropped a Bridge on Him and Conversation Casualty , though the two tropes can go hand-in-hand with this one. Can also be considered a Sub-Trope of Harbinger of Impending Doom but is generally a plot twist. As in, up to this point everything has been going in the protagonist's favor up until this trope is invoked. Also compare with Dynamic Entry , which is usually nonfatal and portrayed in a lighter tone . In the Back and Accidental Murder may use this. Killed Mid-Sentence is a specific sub-trope; Distracted from Death and Surprise Car Crash may overlap. Contrast Dice Roll Death , where a character is killed by bad luck. Not to be confused with the film Sudden Death .
